content.text: *"Let all things be done decently and in order."*{1 Corinthians 14:40}{Programs must be written for people to read, and only incidentally for machines to execute.}{Harold Abelson}

**Pseudocode** is a notation for expressing 
algorithms that is more structured than plain English but less formal 
than a programming language. It allows us to focus on logic without 
worrying about syntax details.

**Assignment**: The statement `x $\gets$ value` assigns 
`value` to variable `x`.

**Return**: The statement `return value` outputs a result and 
terminates the algorithm.

content.title: Sum of array elements
content.problem:
verbatim
 Algorithm SUM(A[1..n])
 Input: Array A of n numbers
 Output: Sum of all elements
 
 total <- 0
 for i from 1 to n do
 total <- total + A[i]
 end for
 return total
verbatim

content.title: Euclidean algorithm for GCD
content.problem:
verbatim
 Algorithm GCD(a, b)
 Input: Non-negative integers a, b (not both zero)
 Output: gcd(a, b)
 
 while b != 0 do
 r <- a mod b
 a <- b
 b <- r
 end while
 return a
verbatim

Trace for GCD(48, 18):

| $a$ | $b$ | $r = a \mod b$ |
| 48 | 18 | 12 |
| 18 | 12 | 6 |
| 12 | 6 | 0 |
| 6 | 0 | (done) |

Output: GCD(48, 18) = 6.

content.title: Euclid's Algorithm (c. 300 BC)
content.content:
The algorithm in Example [ex:euclidean-gcd] appears in Book VII of 
Euclid's *Elements* and is one of the oldest known algorithms still 
in common use. It is remarkably efficient, requiring at most 
$O(\log(\min(a,b)))$ steps. Euclid described it geometrically, finding 
the greatest common measure of two line segments.

content.text:
A **recursive algorithm** calls itself with smaller 
inputs. Every recursive algorithm needs:

items: **Base case(s)**: inputs for which the answer is immediate.
**Recursive case**: the algorithm calls itself with reduced input.

content.title: Recursive factorial
content.problem:
verbatim
 Algorithm FACTORIAL(n)
 Input: Non-negative integer n
 Output: n!
 
 if n = 0 then
 return 1
 else
 return n * FACTORIAL(n - 1)
 end if
verbatim

content.title: Recursive Fibonacci
content.problem:
verbatim
 Algorithm FIB(n)
 Input: Non-negative integer n
 Output: n-th Fibonacci number
 
 if n <= 1 then
 return n
 else
 return FIB(n-1) + FIB(n-2)
 end if
verbatim

This is correct but inefficient—it recalculates the same values many 
times.

your fields ready; after that, build your house.
